ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Aloo Gobhi Sialkoti; closest_parent used for potato and cauliflower dish category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Aloo Gobhi; A0CKP (potato-based dish) used as closest_parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Indian vegetable curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Aloo Gobhi Sialkoti is approximately 250 g, representing one main-course portion of cauliflower and baby potato curry.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Gobhi (Potato and Cauliflower Curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for standard Aloo Gobhi (potato and cauliflower curry). The Sialkoti variant uses the same core ingredients with a slightly drier, spice-forward preparation typical of Sialkot-style cooking. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN standard Aloo Gobhi entry; macros are consistent with the ingredient profile. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as cross-reference for cauliflower (FDC ID 169986) and potato (FDC ID 170026) raw ingredient values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Gobhi Sialkoti. GI estimated at 50 based on analogous mixed potato-vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Boiled potato GI is approximately 78 (high), but the presence of cauliflower (GI ~15), oil, spices, and the curry matrix substantially lowers the overall dish GI. Mixed vegetable curry with potato analogues in the Sydney GI database cluster around GI 45–55. GI 50 (low category, ≤55) is used as a conservative central estimate. GL per serving = (50 × 9.8 × 250) / (100 × 100) = 12.25, rounded to 12.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving provides approximately 525 mg sodium; individuals managing hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to keep daily sodium within the 2000 mg target recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from baby potatoes with low estimated GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 50 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 12 per serving, Aloo Gobhi Sialkoti is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions; the high fibre and fat content from cauliflower and cooking oil further attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based ingredients, and beneficial spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo Gobhi Sialkoti is a plant-based dish with low saturated fat (approximately 0.5 g per 100 g) and contains turmeric and cumin which have anti-inflammatory properties; it is generally suitable for cardiovascular health when prepared with minimal added oil and salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Gobhi Sialkoti is a North Indian vegetable curry originating from the Sialkot region of Punjab, Pakistan. It features cauliflower (gobhi) and baby potatoes (aloo) cooked in a spiced tomato-onion gravy with ginger, garlic, cumin, turmeric, coriander powder, and green chilli. The Sialkoti style is characterised by a relatively dry, intensely spiced preparation compared to the wetter gravies of other regional variants, with the spices being bloomed in oil before the vegetables are added and simmered until tender. The dish has a warm, earthy flavour with mild heat, a slightly crisp exterior on the cauliflower florets, and soft, yielding baby potatoes. It is entirely plant-based, gluten-free, and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (approximately 82 kcal per 100 g), low saturated fat, useful dietary fibre (2 g per 100 g), and meaningful amounts of vitamin C, potassium, and folate from the cauliflower and potato. The estimated glycemic index of 50 places it in the low-GI category, making it appropriate for blood glucose management when portion-controlled. In Singapore, this dish is served at North Indian restaurants such as Kinara at Boat Quay and is typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice. It is a common main-course vegetarian option in Indian restaurant menus across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is Aloo Gobhi good for diabetics?
How many calories in Aloo Gobhi?
What is the difference between Aloo Gobhi and Aloo Gobhi Sialkoti?
What spices are in Aloo Gobhi?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Aloo Gobhi Sialkoti by name; values adapted from the standard Aloo Gobhi entry in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw ingredient data for cauliflower and potato. The Sialkoti variant shares the same core ingredient profile. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database mixed vegetable curry cluster. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted nutrient source and estimated GI.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN standard Aloo Gobhi entry; no exact Sialkoti-specific entry exists in any approved primary source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this dish contains no significant vitamin D sources (no fish, eggs, or fortified dairy); confirmed zero by ingredient composition. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for this specific dish is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_size_g) / 10000 = (50 × 9.8 × 250) / 10000 = 12.25, rounded to 12.3. Source restaurant: Kinara - Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data, 1 restaurant). Price SGD 12.50.

Estimated GI at 50 using analogous mixed vegetable curry entries from University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation.
Documented all zero and -1 sentinel values in notes field.

Used only allowed ontology match_type values: exact, closest_parent, none.
